content.text: Deprecated Model Sunset and Migration Process

When Anthropic moves a Claude model version from the approved matrix to the DEPRECATED stage, the clock starts on a mandatory migration. Deprecated versions remain accessible via the API for a limited window — typically 90 days from the deprecation notice — before reaching End of Life (EOL), at which point API calls to that model ID return an error. The sunset and migration process is the organisation's response to this countdown: identifying every integration using the deprecated version, planning and executing the migration, and verifying that no production traffic reaches the model ID on or after its EOL date.

attrs.alt: Model version deprecation and sunset timeline showing four phases: Active Support, Maintenance, Deprecation Notice (90-day window), and End of Life

content.text: Identifying All Affected Integrations

The first task when a deprecation notice arrives is a complete audit of where the deprecated model version appears in the codebase and infrastructure. Model IDs appear in at least four locations: source code (direct API calls), configuration files (environment variables, YAML, JSON config), infrastructure-as-code templates (ECS task definitions, Kubernetes ConfigMaps), and CI/CD pipelines (test scripts that call the API). Each location requires a different search strategy, and each has different migration mechanics.

The migration_complete method returning True is the condition checked by the deployment gate before the EOL date. If any occurrence remains, the deployment should be blocked until the migration is verified. Running the audit as a CI check on every PR in the lead-up to the EOL date gives the team continuous visibility: the total_locations count decreasing over time shows migration progress, and a sudden increase (someone added a new reference to the deprecated model) triggers an immediate review.
Migration Risk Assessment and Sequencing

Not all integrations carry the same migration risk. An internal batch processing job that produces summaries carries lower risk than a customer-facing API endpoint that is called in real-time by paying customers. A service that makes 100 API calls per day is easier to roll back than one that makes 500,000. The migration plan sequences integrations by risk: low-risk integrations migrate first to build confidence in the replacement model's behaviour, and high-risk integrations migrate last with the most preparation.
Risk Factor
Low Risk
High Risk
Traffic volume
< 1,000 calls/day
> 100,000 calls/day
User visibility
Internal tool, batch job
Customer-facing, real-time
Output strictness
Free text, no parsing
Structured JSON, parsed downstream
Rollback difficulty
Config change, no downtime
Requires redeployment and canary window
Downstream dependencies
No downstream consumers
Other services consume this output

The sequencing principle: migrate low-risk integrations in the first 30 days of the 90-day window to validate the replacement model, migrate medium-risk integrations in days 30–60, and migrate high-risk integrations in days 60–80. Days 80–90 are a buffer for issues and rollbacks, not an extended migration slot. Starting a high-risk migration at day 85 is not a plan — it is a forced choice under time pressure, which increases both the probability of errors and the difficulty of recovering before EOL.
Executing the Model ID Substitution

The mechanical part of most migrations is straightforward: replace the deprecated model ID string with the replacement model ID. The complexity arises from the cases where a simple string replacement is not sufficient — where the replacement model's behaviour differences require accompanying changes to system prompts, parsing logic, or error handling.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable

@dataclass
class ModelMigrationPlan:
    deprecated_id: str
    replacement_id: str
    requires_system_prompt_update: bool
    requires_output_parser_update: bool
    requires_tool_schema_update: bool
    validation_steps: list[str]
    rollback_model_id: str    # the model to fall back to if migration fails

    def migration_tasks(self) -> list[str]:
        tasks = [f"Replace model_id: {self.deprecated_id!r} -> {self.replacement_id!r}"]
        if self.requires_system_prompt_update:
            tasks.append("Update system prompt for new model instruction-following style")
        if self.requires_output_parser_update:
            tasks.append("Update output parser for changed response format")
        if self.requires_tool_schema_update:
            tasks.append("Verify tool call schemas against new model's tool-use contract")
        tasks.extend(self.validation_steps)
        return tasks

    def is_simple_substitution(self) -> bool:
        return (
            not self.requires_system_prompt_update
            and not self.requires_output_parser_update
            and not self.requires_tool_schema_update
        )

def build_migration_plan(
    deprecated_id: str,
    replacement_id: str,
    capability_diff: dict,
) -> ModelMigrationPlan:
    """
    capability_diff keys: changed_instruction_following, changed_output_format,
    changed_tool_schema, validation_checklist (list of strings)
    """
    return ModelMigrationPlan(
        deprecated_id=deprecated_id,
        replacement_id=replacement_id,
        requires_system_prompt_update=capability_diff.get("changed_instruction_following", False),
        requires_output_parser_update=capability_diff.get("changed_output_format", False),
        requires_tool_schema_update=capability_diff.get("changed_tool_schema", False),
        validation_steps=capability_diff.get("validation_checklist", []),
        rollback_model_id=deprecated_id,
    )

The is_simple_substitution method identifies migrations that can be merged as a single-line PR change versus migrations that require a multi-file change with accompanying tests. Simple substitutions can be batched across multiple services in a single sprint; complex substitutions with system prompt or parser changes need dedicated implementation and testing time per service. The migration plan produced by build_migration_plan is created early in the 90-day window — not after the sprint planning for the migration sprint — so the team knows in advance which integrations require significant work.
Verifying Migration Completeness Before EOL

The final gate before the EOL date is a verified-complete audit: every occurrence of the deprecated model ID must be replaced and confirmed to be functioning in production on the replacement version. This is not a code search — it is a runtime verification. A service may have the deprecated model ID removed from its source code but still be running on a container image built before the migration (cached image, manual deploy that was skipped, feature flag that routes to the old version). The completeness check must include production telemetry, not just code search.
Code search:
Run the audit script against the current HEAD of every service repository. Zero occurrences of the deprecated model ID in source, config, and IaC must be confirmed.
Deployed image verification:
Confirm that the running container images for all services were built from post-migration commits. Image digests should be captured from the deployment platform and matched against the commit SHAs.
Production API traffic logging:

Check API request logs from the past 24 hours for any calls to the deprecated model ID. If the deprecated model still appears in production logs, the service using it has not been fully migrated — find it and migrate it before the EOL date.
Canary smoke test:

Run a golden-set smoke test against every service on its replacement model version, exercising the integration in production conditions. A service that passes code review but fails its smoke test on the replacement model was not adequately tested during the migration sprint.
Sign-off record:

Capture the completion of each verification step in the deprecation audit record with a timestamp and the name of the engineer who confirmed it. The sign-off record is the evidence that due process was followed if questions arise after the EOL date.
Tracking Sunset Progress Against the 90-Day Window

The 90-day migration window has a natural structure: it starts wide (many integrations unmigrated) and must converge to zero before day 90. Managing this convergence requires a tracking mechanism that shows progress at the integration level, not just the percentage. An aggregate metric like "60% migrated" conceals whether the remaining 40% consists of easy config changes (days of work) or complex high-traffic services (weeks of work). The tracking system must expose both the count and the risk profile of unmigrated integrations.

The unverified_by_risk method is the most actionable metric in the dashboard. A completion_rate of 0.90 with 5 unverified "high" risk integrations remaining is a critical situation with 14 days left; the same completion_rate with 5 unverified "low" risk integrations remaining is manageable. The at_risk method surfaces integrations that need immediate attention, and is_at_risk uses risk-tier-aware thresholds — high-risk integrations trigger the at-risk flag with 14 days left, while low-risk integrations only trigger it at 3 days, reflecting the different lead times required to safely migrate each category.
Handling Services That Cannot Meet the EOL Deadline

In rare cases, a service legitimately cannot complete migration before the EOL date: the replacement model produces materially different output for a critical use case and the system prompt changes required to compensate need extensive testing, or the service is undergoing a major architectural change that makes a model migration mid-project too risky. The correct response is not to silently miss the deadline — it is to escalate early and obtain an explicit exception through a formal process.
Request an exception at day 30, not day 85:

An exception request submitted with 60 days remaining gives the programme manager time to evaluate options (extended support from Anthropic, a temporary API proxy that redirects calls to the replacement model, or a dedicated sprint). An exception request at day 85 typically means a forced migration with insufficient testing — which is worse than the original deadline miss.
Document the specific blocker:

The exception request must describe exactly what blocks migration: which golden-set prompts fail on the replacement model, what behaviour difference is observed, and what system prompt changes have been attempted. Vague requests ("the migration is complex") are not sufficient. Anthropic support may have specific guidance for the observed behaviour difference.
Define the alternative path:

Every exception must include an alternative completion date and a concrete plan to reach it. A 14-day extension with a specific sprint plan and an identified owner is acceptable. An open-ended extension without a plan is not — it shifts the same deadline risk to the future with added complexity.
Engage Anthropic support:

If the blocker is a model behaviour regression on the replacement model, open a support ticket with Anthropic describing the observed difference, the prompt that triggers it, and the output from both model versions. Anthropic may confirm the new behaviour is intentional (and provide system prompt guidance) or identify it as a bug with a workaround. Either resolution unblocks the migration faster than iterating without support input.
Post-EOL Verification and Incident Response

On the day of the EOL date, the deprecated model ID becomes unavailable. API calls to it return an error response — typically a 404 Not Found or a specific error code indicating the model is no longer available. Even after a thorough migration, some calls to the deprecated model ID may appear on EOL day: requests in flight during the transition, cached routing configurations not yet flushed, or integrations believed to be migrated that were in fact running on a stale container image. The incident response plan for EOL day treats any such calls as service-affecting incidents requiring immediate resolution.

The KNOWN_ROOT_CAUSES dictionary encodes the four common root causes of EOL-day incidents and their resolutions. Having this reference ready in the incident runbook prevents teams from spending time diagnosing causes that are already well-understood. The mttr_minutes method tracks Mean Time to Resolution for EOL incidents — a metric that feeds into the retrospective. An MTTR above 30 minutes for a "stale_image" root cause indicates the team did not have deployment runbooks ready, which is a process gap to fix before the next deprecation cycle.
Retrospective and Policy Improvement After Each Sunset

Each completed model version sunset is a data point for improving the next one. The retrospective is not optional even when the migration went smoothly — a smooth migration that took 85 of 90 days left little margin, and the next deprecation cycle might run concurrently with a major product launch. The retrospective has four agenda items: timeline adherence, exception requests, EOL-day incidents, and policy updates.
Agenda Item
Question to Answer
Output
Timeline adherence
Which integrations missed their target migration window? Why?
Updated risk-tier thresholds; earlier start dates for high-risk services
Exception requests
Were any exceptions needed? Was the blocker avoidable?
Updated golden-set coverage for the behaviour that triggered the blocker
EOL-day incidents
Were there any API errors on or after the EOL date? Root cause?
Runbook updates; deployment checklist additions
Policy updates
Should any process steps be added, removed, or resequenced?
Updated deprecation runbook version; shared to all service owners

The retrospective output is a versioned update to the deprecation runbook. Each model version sunset produces a runbook iteration; over several cycles the runbook converges on a process that handles the organisation's specific integration patterns accurately. Sharing the updated runbook with all service owners after each retrospective ensures that the team responsible for a service that was easy to migrate this cycle is prepared for the harder migration they might face next time.
Summary

Deprecated model sunset and migration is a time-boxed process with a hard deadline. The 90-day window begins when the deprecation notice is published and ends at the EOL date when the deprecated model ID stops accepting API calls. Success requires identifying all affected integrations across source code, configuration, and infrastructure; sequencing migrations by risk (low-risk first, high-risk last); executing model ID substitutions with any required accompanying changes to system prompts or parsers; and verifying completeness through both code search and production telemetry before the deadline. Organisations that treat the migration as a sprint-scoped engineering task with explicit ownership, a structured rollback plan, and verified runtime confirmation will consistently complete sunsets with zero EOL outages.
